video_title: Can We Trust This US-Iran "Peace Deal"? | Michael Every
gist: Rabobank global strategist Michael Every argues the just-announced US-Iran 'peace deal' is most likely a fragile memorandum of misunderstanding rather than a resolution, assigning sub-50% odds that Iran has actually agreed to give up uranium enrichment, and frames the entire episode as a poker game over Middle East energy and China supply chains whose ambiguous outcome ('a messy draw') keeps every regional and dollar-system pivot in play. He ties the 60-day negotiating window to the November US midterms, expects Trump to escalate geopolitics after them regardless of result, and reiterates his long-standing thesis that globalization is being permanently replaced by nationalism, mercantilism and trading blocs.

insights.claim: Michael Every assigns less than a 50% probability that Iran has actually agreed to give up uranium enrichment, meaning the greater-than-50% case is that the core nuclear crisis remains unresolved despite the announced peace deal.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Markets pricing a durable resolution are likely mispricing tail risk of re-escalation; fade the peace-deal relief rally.
insights.quote: That still strikes me as less than a 50% probability.

insights.claim: The only side releasing details of the US-Iran agreement is Iran's Fars news service, which describes terms identical to a deal Trump vociferously rejected weeks earlier before resuming bombing, making the deal's authenticity highly suspect.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: An information vacuum filled only by one side's propaganda means headline-driven trades carry elevated reversal risk.
insights.quote: the deal is exactly the same one that Trump viferously rejected when they flagged it a few weeks ago and ended up bombing them again

insights.claim: Every characterizes the announced framework as at best a loose, performance-based 'you do this if we do that' memorandum of misunderstanding with no guarantee the nuclear core is ever addressed, rather than a binding peace agreement.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Treat the 'deal' as optionality, not settlement; the sequencing risk keeps energy and geopolitical volatility live.
insights.quote: the words peace and deal are doing a lot of very heavy lifting in that particular sentence

insights.claim: Trump has already told the New York Times that if Iran does not agree on the nuclear issue after 60 days of negotiations he will resume bombing and may seek a 20% cut as overseer of the Middle East's oil.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: An explicit bombing-and-oil-cut threat embedded in the deal signals the ceasefire is conditional and reversible within months.
insights.quote: if they don't after 60 days negotiations agree to anything on the nuclear front, then of course I start bombing them again and I may end up being the overseer of the Middle East's oil for which I want a 20% cut

insights.claim: Negotiations between the US and Iran remain primarily bilateral but with Pakistan acting as the sole middleman because the two sides will not talk directly, introducing interpretation risk from the mediator.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Reliance on a single mediator who may massage messages raises the odds of misunderstanding-driven breakdowns.
insights.quote: it's still primarily birectional with Pakistan actually then being the middleman talking to both sides because they won't talk to each other directly

insights.claim: Every notes uncertainty over who in Iran the US is actually negotiating with, comparing it to the US negotiating with Gavin Newsom, who is eloquent and promising but holds no national power.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A fractured Iranian counterparty means apparent agreements can be repudiated by hardliners like the IRGC, undermining any deal.
insights.quote: imagine if Iran was negotiating with Gavin Newsome. That that could potentially be what we're talking about here.

insights.claim: Every dismisses the prospect of all regional parties signing the Abraham Accords as part of this deal as essentially impossible.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Discount any market narrative premised on a sweeping regional normalization wave following the Iran deal.
insights.quote: I just saw two pigs going past the window there. Okay. Um, yeah, that's that's that's not going to happen.

themes.theme: Hormuz, oil curves and energy plumbing

insights.claim: Oil fell only about 4% on the day of the peace-deal announcement, a small move that signals the spot market sees little remaining Hormuz risk and implies bigger reactions will surface in other parts of the curve and related assets.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A muted spot reaction points traders toward curve shape, bond yields and second-order plays rather than outright crude.
insights.quote: down around 4% or something today, which is pretty small considering that peace in our time

insights.claim: Every expects that if Hormuz reopens, a backed-up queue of ships will release a one-way wave of product, putting further near-term downward pressure on energy prices and therefore on bond yields.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A reopening could trigger a transient oil glut and lower yields, a window for energy-short and duration-long positioning.
insights.quote: a lot of ships if they are allowed out will form a queue to get out which will bring a lot of product with them in a one-way wave

insights.claim: Every argues the 60-day reopening window may produce only a temporary flood of tankers that turn back toward Hormuz as the window's expiry nears, so the strait may never properly reopen and the episode could amount to a one-off hostage exchange plus lower midterm gas prices.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Any energy relief may be a brief head-fake rather than structural; positioning should account for a snap-back.
insights.quote: But that doesn't mean that Hormos actually reopens properly in the interim. you just get a one-off hostage exchange and then we see where we go from there.

insights.claim: Every says Gulf states such as the UAE and Saudi Arabia will not shelve their Hormuz-bypass plans after a deal but will instead accelerate alternative pipelines (Fujairah eastward; routes west via Jordan, Syria, Turkey, and contentiously Israel-Cyprus-Greece) to ensure they can never be pressured the same way again.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A multi-year pipeline and midstream buildout is coming regardless of the deal, a durable theme for energy infrastructure exposure.
insights.quote: Everybody will be looking at building whatever infrastructure they can to make sure that regardless of what happens next

insights.claim: Every sees a realistic possibility that GCC states fund a Western naval flotilla and massive investment in anti-drone and anti-missile defenses to make the Gulf a defensive 'porcupine' deterring Iran from ever closing Hormuz again.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Points to sustained Gulf defense-spending demand for missile-defense and counter-drone suppliers.
insights.quote: massive investment in anti- drone uh and anti-missile technology to make sure they are

insights.claim: Iran's ability to close Hormuz in future is being structurally weakened because the bypass infrastructure and deterrents being built leave it less leverage to make trouble, a dynamic Iran itself recognizes.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Iran's energy-chokepoint optionality decays over time, gradually lowering the geopolitical risk premium in oil.
insights.quote: it would actually be in a much weaker position going forward to try and be a troublemaker um in that there's less trouble it can make

insights.claim: Every says there is essentially no chance Iran will agree to defund its terror proxies because it views Hezbollah and others as both a defensive shield and an offensive weapon, leaving a live conflict layered on top of the deal.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: An unresolved proxy conflict is a standing detonator for the whole framework; watch Lebanon for breakdown triggers.
insights.quote: Not going to happen, but very very important.

insights.claim: Iran has explicitly tied the Israel-Hezbollah conflict in Lebanon to the US-Iran talks, and the original deterrence has flipped so that Iran is now stepping in to protect Hezbollah from Israel rather than Hezbollah deterring attacks on Iran.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Linking Lebanon to the nuclear track means a flare-up there can collapse the broader deal, raising contagion risk.
insights.quote: Iran has very very explicitly now tied the outcome of what's going on between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon to what's happening between the US and Iran

insights.claim: Israel's post-October-7 strategic doctrine of preemptively striking any perceived threat makes it a non-starter for Israel to accept US demands to hold fire while Hezbollah rearms, threatening the deal's durability.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: US restraint on Israel is unsustainable; expect Israeli strikes that could rupture the ceasefire framework.
insights.quote: Israel's entire strategic doctrine post October the 7th is they will no longer have a wait and see approach. Anything they see that looks like a potential problem, they hit first.

insights.claim: Every argues that if the US ties Israel's hands against Hezbollah and Iran, Trump could ironically trigger regime change in Israel because Netanyahu, already trailing badly in polls before a September-October election, could not frame the outcome as a victory.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Israeli political instability is an under-watched swing factor; an election loss could reset regional risk posture.
insights.quote: He may ironically end up with regime change in Israel because you have an election day coming up

insights.claim: Every argues Iran has been physically pulverized but that its underground bases and missile systems proved far more survivable than expected, a partial military win demonstrating significant indigenous defense technology despite sanctions.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Proven hardened-bunker and missile resilience means a purely military 'win' over Iran is harder than headlines imply, sustaining risk.
insights.quote: its missile systems and its underground bases, etc., that it's spent a lot of GDP putting in place have been much more successful than people would have thought

insights.claim: Iran's economy was already a shambles before the war, with Tehran close to running out of drinking water from economic mismanagement, and a forecast super El Nino drought could compound the crisis.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Iran's underlying economic fragility limits its ability to project power sustainably, capping any 'Iran is winning' narrative.
insights.quote: you already had tan close to running out of drinking water due to economic mismanagement which has nothing to do with the US

insights.claim: Every contends the US could end the war and force Iranian capitulation overnight by destroying Iran's bridges, power plants, water infrastructure and reinforced uranium bunkers like Pickaxe Mountain, but chooses not to for moral and political reasons.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The decisive military asymmetry favors the US, so Iranian 'victory' is conditional on Western restraint, not capability.
insights.quote: The reality is that the US has that power. Iran doesn't.

insights.claim: Every assesses Iran as both a significant victor in moral and social-media standing and an enormous loser economically, declining to declare an overall winner until the deal's real terms emerge over the coming months.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The win/loss verdict is unresolved, reinforcing that the situation is a 'messy draw' rather than a tradeable resolution.
insights.quote: Iran has been a significant victor in some contexts and an enormous loser in others and you have to look at the win loss column

themes.theme: US-China poker over energy supply chains

insights.claim: Every reiterates his early thesis that the Iran confrontation was never just about nuclear weapons but about controlling Middle East energy and the raw-material supply chain to China, giving the US upstream leverage against China's downstream production dominance.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Frames Iran as a proxy front in US-China competition; the real stakes are commodity supply-chain control, not just proliferation.
insights.quote: this wasn't just about Iran, it wasn't just about the nuclear weapon. It was about energy in the Middle East and it was about the fact that there's a supply chain of raw materials from the Middle East to China.

insights.claim: Every argues China has played a strong card by sharply cutting oil imports, which is a key reason crude is not at triple digits, but says this reflects private 'teapot' refiners running down stock on poor crack spreads rather than a permanent demand shift, meaning China has kicked the can rather than solved the problem.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: China's import pullback is finite (reserves and run-down stocks), so suppressed oil prices could reverse, watch Chinese refinery restocking.
insights.quote: they haven't solved the problem. They've kicked the can down the road.

insights.claim: Every says China has demonstrated it can ride out an oil-supply squeeze for up to six months, forcing the US to rethink, back off and replan with better counter-drone technology and a 2027 UAE pipeline rather than escalate immediately.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: China's demonstrated six-month staying power resets the timeline for any US energy-chokepoint strategy to multi-year.
insights.quote: China has shown, look, we can ride this out for up to six months. So, if you're ever going to try this with us, you better be able to write this for six months, not six weeks.

insights.claim: Every floats a US energy-bloc concept he calls NAPHA (North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Hub Association) uniting Canada, the US, Mexico, Venezuela, Guyana and other Latin producers so the bloc has all the oil and refining it needs while the rest of the world faces $200 oil.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A Western-Hemisphere energy bloc is a radical option that would bifurcate global oil pricing, a long-tail scenario for energy and refiners.
insights.quote: instead of a NAFTA, you would have NAPFA, which is the North American Petroleum and Hydro Harbons Trading Hub Association

insights.claim: Every says the US likely expected a quick Venezuela-style outcome in Iran within days to a week or two but lost that gamble when it did not materialize, weakening the US position in the geopolitical 'poker game'.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A failed quick-win bet signals the US must regroup before its next geopolitical move, extending the strategic timeline.
insights.quote: they've lost that particular gamble if you're looking at this who has the cards casino game of poker

insights.claim: Every confirms the view that US moves on Venezuela and Iran ahead of the Trump-Xi meeting were a deliberate, more muscular strategy to signal to China that Washington could connect supply-chain dots.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Reads recent US actions as signaling theater toward China, implying further leverage plays around supply chains.
insights.quote: 100%. And of course, I think that was with the expectation on the US side that um this would be a Venezuela style outcome

insights.claim: Every argues an inconclusive Iran outcome makes it an urgent imperative for the US to score a quick win in its own hemisphere, and he assigns 'high' probability to material regime or relations change with Cuba.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Flags Cuba as a near-term US target; watch for escalation and shipping interdiction in the Caribbean.
insights.quote: if I was Cuba, I'd be sleeping very uneasily.

insights.claim: Every says Latin American elections are tipping the US way by fine margins, citing Peru, an upcoming Colombian vote, and Carney's pivot in Canada from a January China-aligned new world order to seeking a US trade deal.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A pro-US political drift in the Americas supports the hemispheric-bloc thesis and US-aligned trade realignment.
insights.quote: we've had Carney pivot from new world order with China in January to how do I do a trade deal with the US to make America great again

insights.claim: Every notes a suggestion that Japan might invest in critical minerals in Greenland with the US standing behind it, and that the US may obtain sovereign-territory military bases there, potentially satisfying its Greenland objectives.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Greenland critical-minerals and base deals are a watch item for the US resource-security and defense agenda.
insights.quote: we have this suggestion that Japan might invest in critical minerals there, which of course will be with the US standing behind them

themes.theme: Dollar, stablecoins and the pivot risk

insights.claim: Every broadly agrees with Brent Johnson's dollar-milkshake view that de-dollarization initiatives are largely lab experiments and that the US will act to protect dollar primacy.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Tempers de-dollarization bearishness; the base case remains continued dollar dominance.
insights.quote: Well, I'm broadly of the same mind on that.

insights.claim: Every warns that if the deal's terms turn out as Iran describes them, with the US leaving the region, tying Israel's hands, giving Iran $300 billion and conceding on uranium, it would be a genuine pivot point that opens a fissure damaging to the US and the dollar story.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A 'US capitulation' scenario is the key trigger to watch for a structural dollar-confidence break.
insights.quote: it's hugely damaging to the US and the dollar story to the point where it starts to open a fissure within which more damage can be done

insights.claim: Every frames US stablecoins as a key part of slicing and dicing the dollar into a new format under a 'reverse perestroika', but warns their adoption fails if the world starts viewing the US as a paper tiger after the Iran episode.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Stablecoin adoption is geopolitically contingent on US credibility; an Iran-driven confidence hit could stall it.
insights.quote: slicing and dicing of the dollar into a new format under a reverse paristrika

insights.claim: Every reasons that because a US-capitulation deal would be so self-damaging and the US can see that as clearly as he can, the actual deal is unlikely to match the morning's headlines and something different will more likely emerge over time.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Argues against trading the worst-case dollar scenario now; expect terms to shift away from Iran's framing.
insights.quote: this is unlikely to be the deal. that the headlines this morning as I'm speaking to you Asia Time

insights.claim: China announced over the weekend that its mBridge project, an alternative cross-border payment system to the US dollar and SWIFT, will go live involving Saudi Arabia.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A live mBridge with a major Gulf oil exporter is a concrete de-dollarization step worth monitoring for adoption.
insights.quote: China over the weekend has announced that the Mbridge project as an alternative payment system to the US dollar and and Swift is going to go live involving Saudi Arabia

insights.claim: Every says the US India-Middle East Economic Corridor (IMEC) designed to link India through the Middle East to Europe and cut China out of trade loops is obstructed by Iran and that the announced deal does not resolve that obstacle.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: IMEC's viability depends on the Iran outcome; the unresolved deal keeps this trade-corridor thesis on hold.
insights.quote: America wanted to link India through the Middle East to Europe as a way to basically cut China out of more trade loops which is called the IMC the India Middle East economic corridor

insights.claim: Every floats that a purported $300 billion Iran reconstruction fund, politically toxic if it came from the US, might instead come from Gulf money and US private capital as part of a secret 'reverse perestroika' that hollows out the Iranian regime while leaving the political shell intact, as happened with China.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A China-style hollowing-out of Iran is a low-probability but high-impact path to regional de-risking and investment opportunity.
insights.quote: that 300 billion is from Gulf money from other sources including some US private sector capital which is part of a secret unofficial paristrika

themes.theme: Mercantilism and the new world order

insights.claim: Every reiterates his long-held high-conviction thesis that globalization is permanently giving way to nationalism and mercantilism, with the one-world system collapsing and being reordered into regional trading blocs.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The structural regime shift toward blocs is the master trend shaping markets, supply chains and policy for years.
insights.quote: the one world system is collapsing and it's now going to be reordered and countries are looking at others to kind of tag onto themselves

insights.claim: Every identifies supply chains, commodities, energy, defense and AI as the key sectors that will define the bloc-building era, with headlines dominated by who builds what, with whom, using which inputs, paid for in which currency.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A direct sector roadmap for the mercantilist era: overweight commodities, energy, defense, supply-chain and AI exposure.
insights.quote: there are certain things which are obviously going to be key to that supply chains commodities energy defense

insights.claim: Every predicts Trump will dial up geopolitical aggression after the November midterms regardless of the result, because winning gives him license and losing means 'the only way out is through', implying more volatility ahead.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Positions late 2026 as a likely escalation window; volatility hedges and event risk rise after the midterms.
insights.quote: If he wins the midterms, he dials it up because he can. if he loses them, the only way out is through. You dial it up.

insights.claim: Every expects the 60-day Iran negotiating window to be extended repeatedly but not much beyond the November midterms, after which the conflict could resume for legacy's sake, making the deal effectively a political pause.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Frames the deal as a clock tied to US electoral timing; expect re-escalation risk to spike after November.
insights.quote: this 60-day window could become extended and extended but not much further than the midterms and then we go back potentially to round two for legacy sake

insights.claim: Every notes that even if the US finishes with Iran and decides to leave the region, it cannot redeploy aircraft carriers elsewhere immediately because it needs a couple of years to rearm, creating a window where other global players probe for better terms or squeeze peripheral targets.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A US rearmament gap means opportunistic geopolitical risk elsewhere over the next couple of years; watch for flashpoints.
insights.quote: even if the US says right, we're going home, they're not ready to send aircraft carriers somewhere else tomorrow. We know that they need a couple of years to rearm
